summary: Jeff Atwood explores various metaphors for software development—from construction to farming to art—and argues that the gardening metaphor best captures how modern software, especially web applications, actually gets built. While Steve McConnell favors the 'construction' metaphor in Code Complete, Atwood contends that software is fundamentally 'soft' and alive, requiring continuous tending rather than one-time building. He emphasizes that the farming metaphor captures the commitment to craft, adaptation to changing conditions, and cyclical nature of software work. The rise of web development, with its frequent deployments and community content management, makes the gardening model especially apt.
key_points: Software development metaphors abound (science, art, process, driving, game, bazaar, construction, farming), but none perfectly captures the reality
The construction metaphor falls short because software is 'soft'—malleable and constantly evolving, unlike buildings
The gardening/farming metaphor captures essential truths: commitment to craft, adaptation to variables, seasonal rhythms, and sustainable growth
Web development particularly fits the gardening model with frequent deployments, organic feature growth, and the need to 'weed' user-generated content
The best software projects feel alive—constantly growing with regular releases and long-term commitment
money_quotes: I think we're leaving one metaphor on the table which more accurately reflects the way software is built in the real world: flail around randomly and pray you succeed by force of pure dumb luck.
All the best software projects I've worked were, for lack of a better word, alive.
The strength of the farming metaphor is the implied commitment to the craft.
Farmers do their best to control those variables, of course, but most of all they must adapt to whatever conditions they're dealt.
Sometimes software is working on its roots. Bolstering its infrastructure. It's growing underground where the public can't see it.
And when you don't weed your garden? The weeds threaten to choke out your crops. Eventually, your software garden looks neglected, and then abandoned.
key_insight: The best metaphor for modern software development isn't construction but gardening—a craft requiring continuous tending, adaptation to changing conditions, and commitment to sustainable growth over time.
